skill.name: azure-storage-file-datalake-py
skill.description: Guides Python developers through Azure Data Lake Storage Gen2 setup, authentication, file system, directory, file, ACL, and async operations.
skill.summary: Use Azure Data Lake Storage Gen2 from Python for hierarchical file systems and analytics storage workflows.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, network, filesystem, and cryptography patterns, but review found a documentation-only skill. The command and system findings are false positives from Markdown fences, inline SDK names, and Python examples. The main residual concern is that copied examples can modify or delete Azure Data Lake resources.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Examples Flagged as Command Execution
low_findings.description: The external command findings point to Markdown code fences, SDK object names, and illustrative Python snippets. Line 15 shows a package installation command for users, not hidden skill execution. No automatic shell execution was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: All cited locations are inside SKILL.md documentation. No script file, hook, or runtime path executes these snippets automatically.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Placeholder Azure URLs
low_findings.description: The network findings are Azure Data Lake endpoint placeholders for the user account. They do not reference an attacker-controlled host or transmit secrets outside Azure SDK usage.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs use the documented dfs.core.windows.net pattern with an account placeholder. No suspicious external collection endpoint appears.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Local File Write Example
low_findings.description: The filesystem finding is a download example that writes to downloaded.txt. It is visible instructional code and does not run automatically or target sensitive paths.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The write occurs in a fenced Python example with a fixed local filename. There is no evidence of covert persistence or data tampering.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptography Blocker
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography finding points to the front matter description block. No weak cryptographic algorithm or cryptographic operation is present at that location.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 3 begins a YAML description field. The reviewed context contains no MD5, SHA1, DES, or comparable weak crypto usage.

low_findings.title: Destructive Azure Storage Operations in Examples
low_findings.description: The skill includes examples for deleting file systems, directories, and files. These are legitimate SDK operations, but users should review names and permissions before copying them into real environments.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The delete calls are explicit and easy to verify. They are not malicious, but accidental use can remove user data.

dangerous_patterns.title: Destructive SDK Calls
dangerous_patterns.description: Examples call delete methods for file systems, directories, and files. These operations can remove Azure Data Lake resources when copied into live code.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The method names clearly perform deletion. The risk depends on user credentials and target resource names.

content.user_title: Build Azure Data Lake Python Workflows
content.value_statement: Python teams often need clear examples for Azure Data Lake Storage Gen2 clients and hierarchical file operations. This skill provides concise SDK guidance for authentication, file systems, files, directories, ACLs, and async usage.
actual_capabilities: Shows installation commands for azure-storage-file-datalake and azure-identity.
Explains service, file system, directory, and file client roles.
Covers creating, listing, uploading, downloading, renaming, and deleting resources.
Includes ACL, metadata, recursive listing, and async client examples.
limitations: Does not run commands, create Azure resources, or validate credentials.
Does not cover every Azure authentication or RBAC troubleshooting scenario.
Examples use placeholder account names and simple resource names.
Production retry, monitoring, encryption, and governance patterns are limited.

use_cases.title: Prototype Data Lake File Operations
use_cases.description: Use the examples to create a small proof of concept for files, directories, and path listing.

use_cases.title: Prepare Analytics Storage Workflows
use_cases.description: Map common ingestion steps to SDK calls for upload, append, metadata, and recursive listing.

use_cases.title: Review Access Control Examples
use_cases.description: Find concise ACL examples before implementing directory-level permissions in Azure Data Lake.

prompt_templates.title: Set Up a Client
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me how to install the Azure Data Lake Python SDK and create a DataLakeServiceClient with DefaultAzureCredential.

prompt_templates.title: Upload and Download Files
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me write a safe Python workflow to upload a local file to ADLS Gen2 and download it back for verification.

prompt_templates.title: Design Directory Operations
prompt_templates.prompt: Plan Python SDK steps for creating nested directories, listing paths recursively, and setting metadata in Azure Data Lake.

prompt_templates.title: Build an Async Ingestion Pattern
prompt_templates.prompt: Draft an async Azure Data Lake Storage Gen2 ingestion approach with credential handling, file clients, uploads, and validation checkpoints.

output_examples.input: Explain how the Azure Data Lake client hierarchy works.
output_examples.output: A short explanation of service, file system, directory, and file clients, with when to use each client.

output_examples.input: Plan a file upload and download workflow for ADLS Gen2.
output_examples.output: A step-by-step workflow covering authentication, target file system selection, upload, download, and verification.

output_examples.input: Summarize ACL operations for a directory.
output_examples.output: A practical checklist for reading permissions, setting owner values, and updating ACL entries recursively.

best_practices: Use least-privilege Azure identities and verify target file system names before write or delete operations.
Prefer hierarchical namespace features for directory-oriented analytics workloads.
Use async clients for high-throughput workflows after adding retries, logging, and validation.
anti_patterns: Do not copy delete examples into production without explicit safeguards.
Do not hardcode real account names, tenant details, or credentials in shared code.
Do not use simple examples as a full production architecture without monitoring and error handling.

faq.question: Does this skill execute Azure commands automatically?
faq.answer: No. It is a documentation skill with examples that a user may adapt manually.

faq.question: Which Python package does it cover?
faq.answer: It covers azure-storage-file-datalake, with azure-identity for DefaultAzureCredential authentication.

faq.question: Does it include async examples?
faq.answer: Yes. It shows the async DataLakeServiceClient import pattern and an async upload and download flow.

faq.question: Can it help with ACLs?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes examples for reading access control, setting permissions, and recursive ACL updates.

faq.question: Does it replace Azure documentation?
faq.answer: No. It is a concise reference and should be paired with official Azure guidance for production systems.

faq.question: What should users verify before using examples?
faq.answer: Users should verify account URLs, file system names, paths, identity permissions, and delete operations.
